learn.title: Solar Power Built Into Buildings
learn.intro: Integrating photovoltaics directly into walls, roofs, and windows turns every building into a power plant, reducing grid demand and carbon emissions. This approach blends architecture with clean energy, making solar invisible yet ubiquitous—a key step toward net‑zero cities.
learn.c1_text: Building‑Integrated Photovoltaics replace conventional building materials with solar cells. Imagine a 10‑story office where the façade produces the same electricity as a 5‑MW rooftop farm, delivering power while still looking like glass or metal.
learn.c2_text: Because BIPV is part of the building envelope, it reduces shading losses and can capture daylight for indoor lighting. Typical systems achieve 12–18 % efficiency, and combined with cool‑roof coatings they lower cooling loads by up to 20 %.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy targets 5 GW of BIPV by 2030. Projects like the Hyderabad IT park have installed 2 MW of façade modules, cutting campus electricity bills by 30 % and earning green‑building credits.
learn.stat2: US DOE: BIPV market projected to reach $12 bn by 2035
learn.stat3: Global BIPV capacity grew 25 % YoY in 2023

learn.title: Comparing Power Costs with LCOE
learn.intro: The Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) translates diverse generation technologies into a single $/MWh figure, accounting for capital, operation, and lifetime output. It lets planners choose the most economical clean‑energy mix for a reliable, low‑carbon grid.
learn.c1_text: LCOE spreads total lifetime costs over total electricity produced. For a 1‑GW solar farm costing $1 bn with 1 500 hours/year output, LCOE ≈ $30/MWh—comparable to a natural‑gas plant at $45/MWh.
learn.c2_text: Capital cost, capacity factor, O&M expenses, and financing rates all influence LCOE. A 10 % reduction in panel price drops solar LCOE by about $3/MWh, while higher capacity factors for offshore wind shave $5–$7/MWh.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023 solar auction recorded an LCOE of $32/MWh, the lowest globally, driven by aggressive tariffs and domestic manufacturing. The government’s 2024 target aims for sub‑$30/MWh for utility‑scale wind.

learn.title: Sodium‑Ion Batteries: Low‑Cost Storage
learn.intro: Sodium‑ion batteries use abundant sodium instead of scarce lithium, promising cheaper, safer grid storage. Their chemistry suits large‑scale applications where energy density is less critical than cost and material availability.
learn.c1_text: Like lithium‑ion, sodium‑ion moves Na⁺ ions between anode and cathode, but uses cheaper electrode materials such as Prussian blue. A 100 kWh system can be built for ≈$70/kWh, roughly 30 % less than comparable lithium‑ion packs.
learn.c2_text: Sodium‑ion delivers 150‑200 Wh/kg (vs 250‑300 Wh/kg for lithium) and operates well at –20 °C to 45 °C. Cycle life exceeds 2 000 cycles, making it ideal for daily‑shift storage in renewable‑rich regions.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of Power funded a pilot 5 MWh sodium‑ion plant in Gujarat, targeting a 20 % cost reduction for rural micro‑grids. The project aligns with the National Energy Storage Mission (2024‑2030).
learn.stat1: World sodium‑ion market forecast: $4 bn by 2030

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Efficiency Leap
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells have surged past 30 % lab efficiencies, promising cheaper, lightweight panels that could eclipse silicon. Their tunable bandgap and low‑temperature processing open doors to flexible and building‑integrated modules.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are crystal structures (ABX₃) that absorb sunlight efficiently. A 1‑cm² lab cell reached 33.5 % efficiency in 2023, rivaling multi‑junction silicon cells that cost >$200/W.
learn.c2_text: High absorption coefficient means thin layers (<1 µm) capture most light, reducing material use. Their bandgap can be tuned from 1.2 to 1.8 eV, allowing tandem stacks with silicon to push efficiencies above 35 %.
learn.c3_text: The Indian Institute of Technology Delhi partnered with a start‑up to install a 500 kW perovskite‑silicon tandem pilot in Delhi, aiming for 25 % lower LCOE than conventional PV by 2026.
learn.stat3: India: 60 GW solar target (2025) – perovskites could supply 5 %

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels: Power From Both Sides
learn.intro: Bifacial modules capture sunlight on the front and rear surfaces, boosting energy yield without extra land. They work especially well on reflective ground or with tracking systems, offering 10‑20 % more power than traditional panels.
learn.c1_text: A bifacial panel with 20 % front efficiency can harvest an extra 5‑10 % from reflected light. On a white‑sand desert, albedo of 0.4 adds roughly 8 % more energy annually.
learn.c2_text: Mounting height, tilt, and ground cover matter. Elevating panels 0.5 m and using a 5° tilt on a reflective gravel bed can raise bifacial gain to 15 %, translating to 1.5 MWh extra per 1 MW plant each year.
learn.c3_text: India’s Gujarat Solar Park installed 300 MW of bifacial modules in 2022, reporting a 12 % higher capacity factor (≈1 950 kWh/kW) versus monofacial peers, helping meet the state’s 30 GW solar goal.
learn.stat1: Global bifacial share: 18 % of new utility‑scale PV (2023)

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment: Finding the Best Sites
learn.intro: Accurate wind resource assessment (WRA) identifies high‑quality sites, de‑risking investments and ensuring turbines operate near their maximum output. Modern tools combine satellite data, LiDAR, and AI to map wind speeds across continents.
learn.c1_text: Global models like ERA5 provide 3‑hourly wind speed maps at 30 km resolution. For a coastal Indian site, average 7 m/s at 80 m hub height translates to ≈2.5 MW per turbine, a 15 % boost over coarse data.
learn.c2_text: LiDAR or meteorological towers record wind over 12‑36 months, capturing turbulence and shear. A 2‑year campaign in Rajasthan revealed 12 % higher capacity factor than initial estimates, justifying a larger turbine selection.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy mandates a minimum 7 % capacity factor for new wind projects. The 2022 National Wind Atlas, built with IRENA support, guides developers to high‑potential zones in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat.
learn.stat3: India: 45 GW wind potential at ≥7 % CF
